body:
### Your current environment

### Environment
**Deployment**
- Docker image: `vllm/vllm-omni:v0.24.0` (container `qwen3tts`), serving `:8091`
- Server reported version: 0.24.0
**vLLM / vLLM-Omni**
- vLLM 0.24.0
- vLLM-Omni 0.24.0
**CUDA / PyTorch**
- PyTorch 2.11.0+cu130 (CUDA build 13.0), CUDA available: yes
- torchaudio 2.11.0+cu130
- GPU: NVIDIA H100 80GB HBM3
**Python libraries**
- transformers 5.12.1
- numpy 2.2.6
- triton 3.6.0
- flashinfer-python 0.6.12
**OS**
- Ubuntu 22.04.5 LTS (container)

### Your code version

vllm-omni 0.24.0


### 🐛 Describe the bug

**Summary**
In `x_vector_only_mode=True` (Base voice clone), generated audio starts with a
substantial, highly variable leading silence — mean ~326 ms, ranging 125–460 ms
across prompts. This inflates perceived time-to-first-audio and makes a fixed
server-side trim hard to tune.

**Environment**
- Qwen3-TTS Base, single-H100 deploy (vllm-omni), 24 kHz output
- Endpoint: `POST /v1/audio/speech`, `stream=false` (non-streaming path, so this
  reflects the model's generated codes, not the streaming/chunk pipeline)
- Reference: `jim.wav` (ref audio's own leading silence is ~20 ms)

**Measurement**
8 prompts × 5 reps = 40 samples. Leading silence = time until audio first crosses
a −34 dB (peak 0.02) energy threshold, 5 ms frame resolution.

| Prompt | leading silence (ms) |
|--------|----------------------|
| Hello there, how are you doing today? | 330 |
| Absolutely, let me check that for you right now. | 340 |
| The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. | 125 |
| Seven. | 380 |
| Thanks for calling, my name is Alex and I can help. | 460 |
| Could you confirm your account number, please? | 375 |
| It looks like your order shipped this morning. | 205 |
| Let's schedule a follow up for next Tuesday. | 390 |

Overall: mean 326 ms · median 358 ms · stdev 101.6 ms · range 125–460 ms.

**Notes**
- Within-prompt stdev is 0 (deterministic; `seed=42`). The variance is entirely
  cross-prompt / content-driven, not run-to-run noise.
- Confirmed on the non-streaming path, so the silence is baked into the generated
  codec frames (not a streaming/chunking artifact).
- For contrast, ICL mode (ref codec prefix + ref_text) shows a much tighter onset,
  since generation continues from in-context speech rather than a cold start.

**Impact**
- Perceived TTFA is worse than the transport TTFA metric suggests (first audio
  bytes are silent).
- A single fixed leading-silence trim threshold must cope with a 125–460 ms

comments.author: IneshReddy249
iancarrasco-b10
comments.body: I'm a bit hesistant about the trimming approach as we don't want to unintentionally trim quiet speech as silence. I feel like it would introduce another silence threshold hyperparameter that would need tuning. This may very well be a structural issue in the model itself. So would be great if we can get more eyes to discuss the high level issue before converging on implementation

comments.author: IneshReddy249
comments.body: Makes sense — I'd rather not add a fragile silence threshold either. Happy to dig into the root cause first: I can run your 40 samples and log where speech actually starts in each one, to see if the silence is consistent per input or changes with a fixed seed. That'll tell us if it's a structural cold-start issue and whether priming is the better fix. I'll hold off on writing code until we've settled the direction.

comments.author: IneshReddy249
comments.body:
Dug into this on a public reference. Two things.

**The silence is in the codes, not the vocoder.** Round-trip (real speech trimmed to start loud → encode → decode) comes back loud at frame 0 (0.397 → 0.330), so Code2Wav isn't muting the opening. Splicing the talker's first generated frames into the middle of a loud sequence still decodes quiet (0.046 / 0.010 vs ~0.2 either side) — quiet wherever you put them. And encoding digital silence gives cb0 `[1995, 215, ...]`, where `1995` is the token every generation opens with. So it's a talker cold-start, and priming is aimed at the right layer.

**But the variance looks like sampler noise, not content.** Ran your 8 prompts × 10 seeds (one ref, global seed varied, onset threshold relative to clip peak):

```
prompt                                yours   median   range   sd
Hello there, how are you doing today?   330      415     470   160
Absolutely, let me check that...        340      347     490   153
The quick brown fox...                  125      477     740   201
Seven.                                  380      337     550   185
Thanks for calling, my name is Alex...  460      395     375   109
Could you confirm your account number?  375      370     590   219
It looks like your order shipped...     205      450     330   112
Let's schedule a follow up...            390      350     470   132
```

Within one prompt, onset swings 330–740 ms across seeds — wider than your entire 125–460 ms cross-prompt range. Medians span 337–477 ms with sd 110–220, not separable. Same under your absolute threshold, so it's not a metric artifact.

Your stdev=0 is fixed-seed repeatability, not stability — five reps at one seed replay one trajectory. So it isn't that some prompts have long silence; any prompt can land ~200–800 ms dependin

comments.author: IneshReddy249
comments.body:
Dug into the seeding. Stage 0 in `deploy/qwen3_tts.yaml` doesn't set `cudagraph_mode` and the header notes the talker runs cudagraph by default; the talker samples at `temperature=0.9, top_k=50, repetition_penalty=1.05`, so this isn't greedy.

`qwen3_tts_talker.py:328-350` says that under full cudagraphs `talker_mtp` loses its per-row generators and runs with a single captured RNG stream, so per-request `tts_local_seed` is not reproducible. A captured stream would replay the same draws on every launch — which would give stdev=0 regardless of the seed, i.e. the determinism may be a cudagraph replay artifact rather than the seed taking effect.

Worth a quick check: same prompt with `seed=42` and `seed=1234`. If the audio is byte-identical, the seed isn't reaching the sampler.

If that's what's happening, the 40 samples are 8 prompts under one fixed noise realization — which fits what I see in-process: varying the seed gives 330–740 ms of onset spread within a single prompt, wider than the 125–460 ms range across your whole table.

comments.author: IneshReddy249
comments.body:
> Ran the priming experiment. Short reference + full ICL substantially reduces both onset and its variance, and the effect is **non-monotonic in reference length** — shorter is better, which was not what I expected.
>
> Setup: 8 prompts × 10 seeds (global seed varied), in-process `qwen_tts`. Reference clips generated by the model itself in x-vector mode, then trimmed to start at speech, so `ref_text` and `ref_code` always match. Onset measured relative to clip peak (10%) rather than a fixed −34 dB, since the absolute threshold turned out brittle near the noise floor.
>
> ```
> config                      frames   median   mean range   mean sd   spk_cos
> x-vector only (baseline)         0      393         502       159      0.982
> A  0.82s "Hi."                  11      178         296        92      0.969
> B  0.79s "Hello there."         10      119         319        98      0.969
> C  1.57s (one clause)           20      331         332       102      0.975
> D  2.61s (full sentence)        33      273         482       152      0.976
> ```
>
> Median onset drops 393 → 119 ms at ~10 frames, and per-prompt sd drops ~40%. Speaker similarity (cosine on the extracted x-vector) barely moves: 0.982 → 0.969.
>
> The non-monotonicity is the surprising part. A ~0.8s / 10-frame prefix is the sweet spot; 20 frames is worse, and 33 frames (2.6s) is close to no priming at all on variance. So this isn't "more in-context speech is better" — it looks like a short, clean, speech-onset-aligned prefix is what constrains the trajectory, and a longer reference gives the model more prosodic context to wander in.
>
> Cost is small: ~10 frames × 16 codebooks = 160 codec tokens plus a short `ref_text`.
>
> **Two caveats.**
>
> Variance is reduced, not eliminated. sd goes 159 → ~95 and rang

title: [Bug]: Large variance in leading silence before speech in Qwen3-TTS  x-vector-only mode
